hero /ˈhɪərəʊ/
noun
  1. a person who is admired for their courage, outstanding achievements, or noble qualities.
    "He was the hero of the city."
[[File:MS-DOS PROMPT.png|thumb|link=|24x24px|left]]BACK-NET [[File:MS-DOS OPTIONS.png|thumb|link=|256x256px]]
[[File:MS-DOS CUSTOM.png|thumb|link=|center|750x750px]]

== Clinical Documentation: '''Hero Syndrome''' == '''Case File ID''': HS-223 '''Prepared by''': Dr. Vernal Febland, Backrooms Red Cross Community '''Date of Report''':---- '''Access Level''': Confidential – Authorized Personnel Only == I. Diagnostic Summary == ''Hero Syndrome'' is a psychological condition in which an individual deliberately instigates or exaggerates dangerous situations with the intent to later intervene as a savior. This behavior is driven by a deep psychological need for validation, recognition, and perceived heroism. Although often mistaken for altruistic behavior, individuals exhibiting this syndrome frequently endanger others to fulfill internalized heroic fantasies. It may go undiagnosed due to the manipulative subtlety and societal admiration for “rescuer” figures. == II. Clinical Characteristics == The following behavioral indicators have been observed in confirmed cases: * Initiation of hazardous or problematic scenarios for self-rescue * Active pursuit of praise or public acknowledgment following an incident * Inconsistent or deceptive recounting of events * A history of involvement in repeated emergencies with unclear origins * Obsessive tendencies regarding heroism, martyrdom, or social status == III. Observational Notes == Neurological scans of selected patients suggest a possible correlation between Hero Syndrome and abnormal dopamine regulation in the mesolimbic reward system. The disorder appears more prevalent in high-adrenaline professions such as firefighting, emergency response, and law enforcement. Ongoing research aims to determine whether trauma exposure or sociopathic traits contribute to symptom onset.
Stupid stupid stupid yellow walls. Although it was difficult to remember my beginning, I promised myself that I would not forget that yellow. If you ask anyone in those useless groups, they will look at you with a nostalgic smile and say, "The beginning of everything, my child...", like a bunch of fools who are constantly trying to romanticize life. But believe me, it was not something to remember with a smile. Was it unique and weird as fuck? Yes. But even the sizzle of those fucking lights can make a person go crazy, even someone like me. Sometimes, when I think about old times, my mind shifts to my old life. They call there "the Frontrooms" around here. I remember when I used to curse at my house's walls and cracked ceiling, repeating the same things every morning. I would set the alarm every night, but I would get up before the alarm every morning. Those times have a great contribution to our intimacy with the ceiling. My ceiling was just as yellow as [[Level 0|those walls]]. I don't like this fact at all. I hated that ceiling, because it got uglier every time I looked at it. It wasn't perfect, there was a mistake about that itself. And if I ever go back there, I will continue to hate it. There are always wrong things. A black t-shirt in white ones. A chocolate is on detergent shelves in a supermarket. But I don't want there to be wrong things in my own life. Because exemplary people like me are meant to be perfect. We just need to support people and be their heroes, and nothing else! There can't be anything problematic in my life, because I'm an exemplary person, right? Of course I am... I don't need your approval. Sometimes when you feel in trouble, you'd look for others, waiting for their support. Exemplary people would become ordinary ones' heroes in their daily life. Heroes would save people. Yes we would...Yes '''I''' would. Don't worry {{USERNAME}}, I will save you.. I will save them all...
'''
''Stupid stupid stupid darkness...''
'''
This time, there were no flickering lights or yellow corridors. We were in a corridor with a dark and humid atmosphere. There were walls around us with plaster cast or bare to the bricks, and more pipes were passing through them than I could count. The liquid voices coming from the pipes were irritating. I approached my accompanist to avoid coming into contact with broken walls or pipes.
'''
''Why are you letting her go first? You should be in the lead...''
'''
She was walking in front. Her name is Aurel-...Aurelie? Or Arielle? Something like that, frankly, I don't really care. She has knotted hair from navigating through the levels. She makes notes in her notebook, whispering measurements, as if they will save us. It's as if data gives you power here. I wanted to tell her that nothing will work, but I'm not saying anything.

'''
''She acts like she is the one who will save you. That's irritating, isn't it?''
'''
That woman did not believe that we are stuck here. Because her stupid little brain was focused only on solutions, not on what the problems were. Her steps were sharp. It was as if she knew where she was going. She was acting like she was going to be the one to save me from this hell I was going through. It was funny. Because I had walked more levels than her. I've seen things, many things more than these pipes. But if I told her these things, she would probably ask me to provide information about the levels I had traveled to, and she would also take notes of them. Her faith in resarch and investigation was almost religious. And it made me itch. Because it was completely following their "member" identity. Big and powerful organizations with big flags and logos. I had interacted with them more than once, but I had not been hired by any of them. I had only one job interview, and it brought to mind the days when I applied for a secretarial position at a large company in my old life. I didn't like things that reminded me of my old life. And the idea that a unique and inexplicable phenomenon should be forced to be simplified and facilitated like the Froontrooms was quite contrary to me. That's why I spoke to groups whose primary focus is not research, but rather helping more travelers. But the leader of the group refused my job request, telling me that "I was too much of a dreamer." and "We need more discoveries to live." [[Jerry M. Sercher|That guy]] is a more genial version of my old boss. That doesn't make him any good. Aurelia slowly stopped while I forced my mind to forget about my former boss's face. Here, the pipes were blocking our way, and we had to play with the placement of some of them somehow. Aurelia was measuring them, taking their temperatures with thermometers and trying to minimize the risk.

'''
''Make her fail. Then step in. That’s how you become necessary.''
'''
She crouched down next to the pipes, lined up her tools on the floor one by one, counting their names. "My father taught me such things when I was little," Oh, HOW CUTE. She was one of those people who talked about their old life with a smile. And I wasn't in a position to deal with the pasts of strangers in this stupid place. Also, she didn't even look back to check on me while talking. I could have disappeared in the dark three minutes ago, and she wouldn't have noticed. Such confidence was one of the main reasons that made her careless. And carelessness would have made her need me. "Hey, Aurelia...That pipe over there...there's no sound of liquid flowing inside, and it's also not hot. Probably empty. If we loosen it up, we can find a way out of the grids above..." "Oh...you mean that one over there? It might be risky, perhaps there is no sound due to a dense liquid." She wasn't finding solutions and wasn't accepting my solutions either. This woman was really getting on my nerves. She continued to look at her notes and examine the pipes for some time. But then she looked at me and spoke in an insecure way. "But it doesn't hurt to try, does it?" "Of course. You must be tired too, Auriele... We really should get out of here now. And then, I'll take you somewhere where you can rest." "Here, there's the pipe... My arm is still injured, otherwise I could have handled it myself. But now you have to do it..." "Alright. I need a screwdriver to do this. and also something sharp..." "Here you go Miss, I've got everything in the bag." I smiled reassuringly, holding her hand gently as I put them into her palm. "Watch my back while I break this useless shit." She looked at me one last time and then set to work on the pipe.

'''
''She is still clueless. And soon you will become her hero...''
'''
If there was ever a moment when my stupid university degree in the Froontrooms would come in handy, this was it. The pipe felt colder than the others, sure—but not naturally cold. It was a cold that resisted ambient temperature. And more than that... the echo. I had tapped it when she wasn’t looking. Expecting a ringing sound. But what I got instead was a low, muffled thud. No high-frequency bounce. No vibrational reverb. Just a dead hum. Gas. And worse, I had seen the hairline crack near the wall joint. It was minute—barely visible. But I recognized the smudged residue around it. A fine powder layer is typical of CO venting. Carbon monoxide. I should’ve said something. But what would that make me? A man who noticed danger and did nothing until it was too late? No... it was better this way. Better to be needed. Better to have her fall—and then reach for me. She was unscrewing the bolts now. Her fingers worked like she’d done this her whole life. 1… 2… 3— Boom. The pipe ruptured like an overfilled vein, a burst of invisible death seeping into the air. The symbol etched on the metal became visible in the flickering light — CO. Carbon Monoxide. Of course. The gas mask in my bag would shield me, just me. Aurelia staggered backward from the blast, her body knocked into an opening. A pit. She slipped. Fell. I didn’t move. Not until I heard it. That fragile sound. “Cassian… help me.” There it was. The line. The proof. Now she needed me. Now I could be the hero.

'''
''What are you standing for? You're a hero. Go and help her.''
'''
Yes. This was my moment... I slowly got out of the back and went to where she was. I bent down to the pit and lifted the body off the ground. Auriele normally didn't like to be touched, but she wasn't making any noise then. "You must be tired, Auriele...Don't worry, you'll have a good rest." I opened the lid of the grate that was left under the pipe we broke, and dragged her inside with me. Maybe we'd end up in a safe place? Or we'd have a cup of tea with friendly entities? I need somewhere safe tho, maybe some group bases... That would be useful. So I could get some rest with her, too. 'Cause you know, even the strongest heroes need a break, right? ''''' Of course...''' ''
They told me to do something noteworthy. It's something useful, different from other people walking around here. This woman in my arms would be valuable enough, wouldn't she?
